---
title: "Agentic AI Course"
---

# Course Objectives

- Design, diagram, implement, evaluate, productize, and scale agentic AI systems using modern LLM frameworks and tooling.
- Identify and evaluate problems that are appropriate for agentic AI solutions, and distinguish them from problems better solved by traditional software or non-agentic LLM approaches.
- Clearly define Agentic AI and related concepts, including agents, tools, planning, grounding, orchestration, evaluation, and human-in-the-loop workflows.
- Develop and articulate ethical guidelines for the use of LLMs and agentic AI, including responsible data usage, AI governance principles, safety, privacy, and organizational policy considerations.
- Build the capability to independently learn, evaluate, and adapt to new AI models, tools, and paradigms in a rapidly evolving AI ecosystem.

---

# Course Overview

## Unit 1: Build a "Bad" Agent (Weeks 1–3)

  - [Unit 1](units/unit1.html)

**Goal:** Learn the tools to create a powerful but uncontrollable agent.

**Topics:**
- Intro to agent concepts and LLM programming
- API keys, environment setup, and class materials
- Making API calls, defining and calling tools, and managing statefulness
- Connecting tools: MCP, file system, browser use, internet search, custom functions
- Sandboxing with LangChain / Daytona
- Intro to subagents and multi-agent architectures

**Project:** Personal Assistant Agent

Build a personal assistant agent that connects to real tools — Canvas, Google Calendar, Slack, the file system, and the web — and carries on a coherent, stateful conversation. You will write your own prompts from scratch; no AI, peer, or internet-generated prompts are permitted. The agent will be stress-tested in class to deliberately surface its weaknesses, setting the stage for Unit 2.

**Outcomes:**
- Call an LLM and receive a response
- Write original prompts for agent context
- Define custom tools and turn existing code into tools
- Connect and invoke tools (MCP, file system, browser, web search, Canvas, Google Calendar, Slack)
- Implement conversation history manually and with built-in methods
- Introduce statefulness to an agent
- Demonstrate a working personal assistant agent and identify where it breaks

**Note on AI use:** AI assistance is not permitted this unit. Debugging help comes from class time and the Slack group (ChatClass). This is intentional — it builds class unity, surfaces your own assumptions, and gives you room to fail. Failure is a core part of this unit, not a grading penalty. Set a time limit on any problem; if you're stuck, post in Slack or bring it to class unfinished. That is OK.

**Daily Schedule:**

| Day | Topic |
|-----|-------|
| 1 | Orientation — meet the professor, course objectives, demo of what you'll build, requirements and expectations |
| 2 | APIs & Your First LLM Call — define an API, make a call, handle common challenges |
| 3 | Tools: Building & Using Them — custom tools, the @ decorator, LangChain Community tools |
| 4 | Statefulness & Conversation History — manual and built-in conversation history |
| 5 | Data & Local Resources — local data, file system access, running code |
| 6 | Connecting to the World — web search, browser use, MCP connections |
| 7 | Sandboxing & Multi-Agent Intro — LangChain/Daytona sandboxing, subagent concepts |
| 8 | Project Kickoff — design the personal assistant, plan tools and personality |
| 9 | Build Day — assemble tools into a working agent |

---

## Unit 2: Diagnostics — Why Is It "Bad"? (Weeks 4–5)

  - [Unit 2](units/unit2.html)

**Goal:** Develop diagnostic tools to peer into the black box of agentic AI, in preparation for building reliable agents.

**Topics:**
- Observability and tracing with LangSmith
- Debugging hallucinations and unexpected behavior
- Token and latency analysis
- Identifying and fixing code bugs

**Project:** Diagnostic Report — Fix a Broken Agent

You are given a broken agent to diagnose. Your job is to implement tracing, identify why it fails, and produce a structured diagnostic report documenting each failure and its root cause.

**Outcomes:**
- Set up LangSmith and capture screenshot traces
- Implement tracing features and explain what each one does and why
- Identify categories of agent failure (context, tool use, looping, cost, etc.)
- Fix identified code bugs
- Produce and present a diagnostic report

**Note on AI use:** AI assistance may be permitted this unit — details TBD. If used, post in Slack what you discussed and any lessons learned.

**Daily Schedule:**

| Day | Topic |
|-----|-------|
| 1 | What Makes an Agent "Bad"? — review Unit 1 failures, introduce diagnostic framing |
| 2 | Observability & Tracing — LangSmith setup, API key, navigating traces |
| 3 | Scavenger Hunt — find specific traces and failure signatures in LangSmith |
| 4 | Analyze a Broken Agent — trace behavior, document what goes wrong |
| 5 | Build the Diagnostic Report — categorize failures, structure findings |
| 6 | Present & Discuss — share reports, find patterns, introduce context engineering |

---

## Unit 3: Context Engineering — Make a "Good" Agent (Weeks 6–8)

  - [Unit 3](units/unit3.html)

**Goal:** Engineer context effectively to produce agents that are reliable, specialized, and capable of operating over long horizons.

**Topics:**
- Context engineering vs. prompt engineering
- The 4 levels of intent: session, user, organization, world
- Simple context management: truncation, summarization, selection, file write, database, isolation/delegation
- Context offloading: semantic search, forgetting vector, graph memory
- Subagent architecture: parallelization, serialization, concurrency
- Checkpointing, human-in-the-loop, and context self-adaptation
- Cost-aware context design

**Project:** Rebuilt Personal Assistant

Take the Unit 1 personal assistant and rebuild it with context engineering applied. Demonstrate measurable improvements in reliability, coherence, and cost efficiency compared to the original version.

**Outcomes:**
- Define context engineering and distinguish it from prompt engineering
- Implement truncation and summarization-based context management
- Apply context offloading using semantic search or graph memory
- Implement parallelized and serialized subagent patterns
- Add checkpointing and human-in-the-loop interrupts
- Compare the rebuilt agent directly to the Unit 1 version

**Daily Schedule:**

| Day | Topic |
|-----|-------|
| 1 | What Is Context Engineering? — definition, levels of intent, why it matters |
| 2 | Simple Context Management — truncate, summarize, select, offload; implement truncation and summarization |
| 3 | Context Offloading — semantic search, forgetting vector, graph approaches |
| 4 | Subagent Architecture — parallelization, serialization, Ralph Wiggum Loop, deep research agent |
| 5 | Checkpointing, HITL & File Systems — messy file navigation, checkpoints, human-in-the-loop |
| 6 | Context Self-Adaptation & Cost Saving — adaptive context, cost reduction review |
| 7 | Project Kickoff — redesign the personal assistant with context engineering applied |
| 8 | Build Day — hands-on build with instructor support |
| 9 | Demo & Compare — showcase rebuilt agent, compare to Unit 1 version |

---

## Unit 4: Productization and Scaling — Make a "Useful" Agent (Weeks 9–11)

  - [Unit 4](units/unit4.html)

**Goal:** Take a working agent and make it production-ready: scalable, safe, observable, and user-facing.

**Topics:**
- Token economics and quadratic cost growth
- Context caps, sliding window, and retrieval-based memory
- Rate limiting, caching, async workflows, and budget controls
- RAG pipelines and knowledge grounding at scale
- Human-in-the-loop workflows and approval flows
- Prompt injection, data leakage, and guardrails
- Frontend interfaces (web/chat)
- Organizational policy and AI governance

**Project:** Battle of the Bots

Design and deploy a production-ready agent of your choice. Agents will compete in a series of structured challenges testing usefulness, resilience, and cost efficiency. Categories include speed, cheapest correct answer, graceful failure, and most improved from Unit 1.

**Outcomes:**
- Explain why cost grows quadratically and how to prevent it
- Implement a context cap and at least one cost reduction technique
- Build and deploy an agent with a real frontend interface
- Harden the agent against prompt injection and misuse
- Apply rate limiting and budget controls
- Implement human-in-the-loop approval flows
- Articulate governance and ethical considerations for production AI

**Daily Schedule:**

| Day | Topic |
|-----|-------|
| 1 | Why Scale Breaks Things — token economics, quadratic cost growth, context windows |
| 2 | Cost Reduction Techniques — sliding window, summary memory, retrieval, saving skills as code |
| 3 | Who Is This Agent For? — defining users, use cases, RAG possibilities |
| 4 | Production Safety & Budget Controls — rate limiting, budget alerts, preventing runaway loops |
| 5 | RAG & Retrieval at Scale — build a RAG pipeline, tune retrieval quality |
| 6 | Hardening the Agent — prompt injection, guardrails, stress testing at scale |
| 7 | Final Agent Design — choose tools, context strategy, and scaling safeguards |
| 8 | Final Build Day — full build session, peer testing and feedback |
| 9 | Battle of the Bots! — structured challenges, audience voting, final showcase |

---

## Capstone (Weeks 12–14)

  - [Capstone](units/capstone.html)

**Final Project:** A production-ready agent system of your own design, incorporating:

- Planning and tool use
- Grounding and memory
- Evaluation and observability
- Safety and guardrails
- A real user interface
- Documentation of design decisions and failure modes encountered
